A design study was made for a reactor system of this type to power a 931 class destroyer of $3 - 4000$ tons displacement.

The reactor and steam generating equipment simply replaced one of the present boiler rooms on this class ship and duplicated the steam conditions $(950^{\mathrm{O}}\mathrm{F}, 1200\mathrm{psig})$ supplied to the propulsion machinery.
An overall specific weight of 59 lbs/SHP was achieved for the 35,000 SHP delivered per boiler room.
This specific weight was achieved with a reactor and steam generating equipment overdesign of approximately $30\%$ .

Indications are that if time had allowed a reiteration of the system size to the $10\%$ overdesign factor used in most reactor systems, a specific weight reduction to at least 54 lbs/SHP would have been achieved.
It was found that through the use of two intermediate loops the amount of secondary shielding could be reduced and the overall specific weight released from 58 to the 54 lbs/SHP.
The comparable reduction for the case with $30\%$ overdesign is from 65 to 59 lbs/SHP.
The reactor and steam generating system were designed to produce 125 MW which is a conservative overdesign of greater than $25\%$ .

An average core temperature of $1225^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ with an $100^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ difference across the core was selected as a compromise of weight and thermal efficiency against corrosion and thermal stress problems.
The neutron flux spectrum is largely intermediate giving rise to a fission distribution of $28\%$ thermal, $63\%$ intermediate and $9\%$ fast.
The reactor vessel itself is approximately 6.7 ft in diameter and 6.7 ft high.
The secondary shield for the basic study enveloped both the reactor and the steam generating equipment and incorporated a thickness of approximately $4 - 6 = 1/2$ inches of lead.

These conditions are 263,300 lb/hr per boiler room at $950^{\mathrm{O}}\mathrm{F}$ and 1200 psi.
In the preliminary analysis of the system it was decided that the reactor power should be 125 megawatts.
Therefore, the steam generating equipment was designed to remove 125 megawatts of heat.

When the actual steam cycle data for the class 931 destroyer was received a complete heat balance revealed that only 95.9 megawatts of heat was necessary to supply the steam for the full power of 35,000 shp.
Detailed calculations of the design specifications are presented in Appendix 7.1.
group was confronted with the problem of removing 125 megawatts of heat from a molten salt coolant.

The temperature of this salt as it leaves the primary heat exchangers is to be $1150^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , and it is to reenter the primary heat exchangers after losing only $100^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ in temperature.
Therefore, to remove the full-power 125 megawatts it is necessary to circulate the molten salt at a rate of $7.49 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{lb/hr}$ .

One of the design criteria is to keep the temperature drop across tube walls below $100^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and since the saturation temperature of water in the boiler is $572^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , then when allowance is made for the boiling water film temperature drop and for the molten salt film temperature drop, it is found that the salt entrance temperature to the boiler should not exceed $800^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The superheater is to bring the steam temperature up from $572^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ to $975^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

Because of the high temperature drop across the steam film an entrance temperature to the superheater of $1150^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and an exit temperature of $1126^{\circ}$ would not exceed the $100^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ drop across the tube walls.

Therefore, the problem is to lower the salt temperature from $1126^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ to $800^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and then to raise it from $734^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , the boiler exit temperature, to $1050^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ in order to return it to the primary heat exchanger.

In the regenerator system the $1126^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ salt would enter and the $1050^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ salt would leave the hot end of the regenerator heat exchanger while $800^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ salt would leave and $734^{\circ}\mathrm{S}$ salt would enter the cold end.
This meant that some 420 megawatts of heat would have to be exchanged in the regenerator.
After consultation it was decided that a blending system would be used thereby allowing the system to consist of a separate hot and cold loop (See Figure 3.1).
On the discharge side of the hot loop pump, molten salt at $1050^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ is tapped off and fed into the cold loop.
The salt flow rates in the superheater and steam generator are to remain constant at 7.49 x $10^{6}$ lb/hr.
The amount of salt bled from the hot loop to the cold loop at full power 125 mw is 1.74 x $10^{6}$ lb/hr.
Schematic layouts of this system are also shown in Figures 7.3 and 10.1.
The fluid horsepower necessary to circulate the molten salt was calculated to be 260 hp in the hot loop and 200 hp in the cold loop.
The calculated pressure drop in the superheater was 15.2 psi, in the
primary heat exchanger 20.5 psi, and in the steam generator 38.7 psi.
Pipes with inner diameters from 7" to 17" were investigated.
The results showed that the optimum pipe i.d. would be approximately 11".
In selecting a steam generator the general types considered were (1) the flash boiler, (2) the once through boiler, (3) the natural circulation, and (4) the forced circulation boiler.

They were: (1) the need for high tube wall $\Delta T$ 's in order to keep salt from freezing on tubes, (2) the lack of nozzles that would give an adequate spray pattern, (3) the need for very long tubes in order to ensure dry steam at high loads, (4) the need for a method to insulate the nozzle headers from the heated tubes, and (5) the general feeling of the group that, although flash boilers show great promise, much more developmental work is needed.

(2) Once-Through Boiler: Once- through boilers have been used in Europe for a number of years and have recently come into their own in this country with the installation of the supercritical units at Philo and Eddystone.
As was stated in Section 7.2 the stresses encountered when boiling water at $572^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ with a salt at $1100^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ introduces intolerable conditions.
(3) Natural Circulation Boiler: The natural circulation boiler is perhaps the conventional steam generator for marine use.

Reports such as KAPL-1450, "Review of SIR Project Model Steam Generator Integrity", seem to indicate that the best results for a liquid metal coolant such as sodium would be obtained by placing the coolant in the tubes with the water on the shell side.
The tubes must also be of the order of 12' to 15' and it was felt that possibly some other arrangement would offer greater compactness.
(4) Forced Circulation Boiler: The forced circulation boiler was selected for the basic study because of its compactness and flexibility of arrangement.
